hints: The acceleration has units of $\mathrm{m/s^2}$ in the SI system, and $g$ has those same units, while $r$ doesn't have any units. This suggests you start by putting $g$ in the first box, outside the fraction. Then fill in the rest of the fraction with $r, 0, 1, +,$ and $-.$ Do this either by completely deriving the formula using energy conservation, by matching what you know happens in the extreme cases, or by matching a formula to the equation you found in the part (A). Remember to use only $+$ and $-$ in the thin spots in the middle of the numerator and denominator. You will need to use a zero.

solution_text:
[b]Solution 1 (heuristic solution)[/b]

We measure accelerations in $\mathrm{m/s^2}$ in the SI system, and we measure $g$ in those same units. $r$ doesn't have any units. This suggests $a \propto g,$ so we put $g$ in front, and then try to fill in the remaining boxes using the $r, +, -, 1,$ and $0.$
[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00405-36293f6558d60af76e24b26cd4d75d9753687fc0/files/04_hw_g_in_front.png[/img][/center]
We saw in part (A) that when $r = 0,$ the mass $m_1$ on top is zero. Then $m_2$ is in free fall, and $a = g.$ This means we want the fraction to be equal to $1$ when $r = 0.$ Another way to say that is that the fraction should be $1$ when we ignore the $r$ variable. To make this happen, we put $1$ in both the numerator and denominator.
[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00405-36293f6558d60af76e24b26cd4d75d9753687fc0/files/04_hw_half_atwood_ones.png[/img][/center]
Next, we saw in part (A) that when $r$ gets bigger and bigger, it means $m_1 \gg m_2,$ and the acceleration should asymptote away towards zero, since the heavy mass $m_1$ on top is getting harder and harder for the lighter hanging mass $m_2$ to pull.

That means we should put an $r$ in the denominator, so that as $r$ grows, $a$ fades towards zero. We don't want an $r$ in the numerator.

We use a plus sign in the denominator to avoid the denominator becoming negative. To fill out the answer, we put $+0$ in the numerator.
[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00405-36293f6558d60af76e24b26cd4d75d9753687fc0/files/04_hw_half_atwood_formula2.png[/img][/center]
[b]Solution 2 (full solution)[/b]

We'll define a system consisting of everything pictured: both masses, the string, pulley, and also the Earth.

Let's imagine that the block of mass $m_2$ falls a distance $d.$

Then the gravitational potential energy of the block on the table doesn't change because it moves horizontally. The gravitational potential energy of the block of mass $m_2$ changes by
$$\Delta U = -m_2 g d.$$

Since energy is conserved, when the gravitational potential energy decreases by some amount the kinetic energy increases by the same amount. The kinetic energy of the system is
$$ egin{aligned}
KE &= - \Delta U \[6pt]
  &= m_2 g d.
\end{aligned}$$

The kinetic energy can also be found by taking the sum of the kinetic energies of the blocks:
$$KE = \dfrac12 \left(m_1 v^2 + m_2 v^2ight),$$
where $v$ is the speed of either block after moving the distance $d.$ (These speeds are the same because the blocks are connected by a rope whose length cannot change.)

Then we have
$$ \dfrac12 (m_1 + m_2) v^2 = m_2 g d.$$

As we saw in class, when an object accelerates from rest with constant acceleration $a$ over a distance $d,$ its speed $v$ is
$$v = \sqrt{2ad}.$$

When we plug this in for $v$ in the previous equation, we get
$$ \dfrac12(m_1 + m_2) \cdot (2ad) = m_2 g d.$$

This simplifies to
$$ a = g \dfrac{m_2}{m_1 + m_2}.$$

Dividing everything by $m_2,$ we get
$$a = g \dfrac{1}{r + 1}.$$

This can be entered as
[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00405-36293f6558d60af76e24b26cd4d75d9753687fc0/files/04_hw_half_atwood_formula2.png[/img][/center]
